In [economics](/wiki/Economics "Economics") and [finance](/wiki/Finance "Finance"), **market manipulation** occurs when someone intentionally alters the supply or demand of a security to influence its price. This can involve spreading misleading information, executing misleading trades, or manipulating quotes and prices.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Market manipulation is prohibited in most countries, in particular, it is prohibited in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") under Section 9(a)(2)[[4]](#cite_note-4) of the [Securities Exchange Act of 1934](/wiki/Securities_Exchange_Act_of_1934 "Securities Exchange Act of 1934"), in the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") under Article 12 of the *Market Abuse Regulation*,[[5]](#cite_note-5) in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") under Section 1041A of the [Corporations Act 2001](/wiki/Corporations_Act_2001 "Corporations Act 2001"), and in [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel") under Section 54(a) of the securities act of 1968. In the US, market manipulation is also prohibited for [wholesale](/wiki/Wholesale "Wholesale") electricity markets under Section 222 of the [Federal Power Act](/wiki/Federal_Power_Act "Federal Power Act")[[6]](#cite_note-6) and wholesale [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas") markets under Section 4A of the [Natural Gas Act](/wiki/Natural_Gas_Act "Natural Gas Act").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

In India it is illegal under the [SEBI](/wiki/Securities_and_Exchange_Board_of_India "Securities and Exchange Board of India") (Prohibition of Fraudulent and Unfair Trade Practices relating to Securities Market) Regulations, 2003.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

## Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Examples")]

### Pools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Pools")]

Main article: [Pool operation](/wiki/Pool_operation "Pool operation")

Agreements, often written, among a group of [traders](/wiki/Merchant "Merchant") to delegate authority to a single manager to trade in a specific [stock](/wiki/Stock "Stock") for a work period of time and then to share in the resulting profits or losses.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In Australia section 1041B prohibits pooling.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

### Runs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Runs")]

When a group of traders create activity or rumours in order to drive the price of a [security](/wiki/Security "Security") up. An example is the [Guinness share-trading fraud](/wiki/Guinness_share-trading_fraud "Guinness share-trading fraud") of the 1980s. In the US, this activity is usually referred to as *painting the tape*.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### Ramping (the market)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ramping (the market)")]

Actions designed to artificially raise the [market price](/wiki/Market_price "Market price") of listed securities and give the impression of voluminous trading in order to make a quick profit.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Bear raid

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Bear raid")]

In a [bear raid](/wiki/Bear_raid "Bear raid") there is an attempt to push the price of a stock down by heavy selling or [short selling](/wiki/Short_selling "Short selling").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Quote stuffing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Quote stuffing")]

[Quote stuffing](/wiki/Quote_stuffing "Quote stuffing") is made possible by high-frequency [trading programs](/w/index.php?title=Trading_program&action=edit&redlink=1 "Trading program (page does not exist)") that can execute market actions with incredible speed. However, high-frequency trading in and of itself is not illegal. The tactic involves using specialized, high-bandwidth hardware to quickly enter and withdraw large quantities of orders in an attempt to flood the market, thereby gaining an advantage over slower market participants.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

### Cross-market manipulation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Cross-market manipulation")]

Cross-market manipulation occurs when a trader trades in one market for the purpose of manipulating the price of an asset in another market, capitalizing off the price-moving effects thus generated, instead of with the bona fide intent of profiting off the trade itself.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

### Cornering the market

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cornering the market")]

Main article: [Cornering the market](/wiki/Cornering_the_market "Cornering the market")

In cornering the market, the manipulators buy a sufficiently large amount of an asset, often a [commodity](/wiki/Commodity "Commodity"), so they can control the price creating in effect a [monopoly](/wiki/Monopoly "Monopoly"). For example, the brothers [Nelson Bunker Hunt](/wiki/Nelson_Bunker_Hunt "Nelson Bunker Hunt") and [William Herbert Hunt](/wiki/William_Herbert_Hunt "William Herbert Hunt") attempted to corner the world [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver") markets in the late 1970s and early 1980s, at one stage holding the rights to more than half of the world's deliverable silver.[[16]](#cite_note-TEXAS-16) During the Hunts' accumulation of the precious metal, silver prices rose from $11 an ounce in September 1979 to nearly $50 an ounce in January 1980.[[17]](#cite_note-NYT-17) Silver prices ultimately collapsed to below $11 an ounce two months later,[[17]](#cite_note-NYT-17) much of the fall occurring on a single day now known as [Silver Thursday](/wiki/Silver_Thursday "Silver Thursday"), due to changes made to exchange rules regarding the purchase of commodities on margin.[[18]](#cite_note-TimeBubble-18)

## New Market Manipulation Examples

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: New Market Manipulation Examples")]

### Pinging and Spoofing

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Pinging and Spoofing")]

Pinging involves making small orders and then cancelling them with a computerized program in an effort to induce others in the marketplace to react to the "pings" and reveal their trading intentions to the party pinging.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Similarly, with spoofing, a computerized program submits or cancelling multiple bids or offers to manipulate the price of a security.

### Electronic Front Running

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Market_manipulation&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Electronic Front Running")]

Similar to traditional [front running](/wiki/Front_running "Front running"), electronic front running uses knowledge of future orders to execute trades ahead of a future price. However, electronic front running utilizes [financial technology](/wiki/Financial_technology "Financial technology") to evaluate price changes and financial transactions for parties to execute advantageous trades.[[20]](#cite_note-20)
